title: Inventory Class — OOP Design
description: Refactor the inventory system into an object-oriented design. Create an Inventory class with a private vector<Product> to store products. Add public methods: addProduct, removeProduct, displayAll, and searchByName. Overload the << operator to display a Product. Keep the Product struct. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.
test_code:
assert 'class Inventory' in cpp or 'class inventory' in cpp, 'Define an Inventory class'
assert 'private' in cpp, 'Inventory class should have a private section'
assert 'public' in cpp, 'Inventory class should have a public section'
assert 'vector<Product>' in cpp or 'vector <Product>' in cpp, 'Use a vector<Product> to store products in the class'
assert 'addProduct' in cpp or 'add_product' in cpp, 'Inventory class needs an addProduct method'
assert 'removeProduct' in cpp or 'remove_product' in cpp, 'Inventory class needs a removeProduct method'
assert 'displayAll' in cpp or 'display_all' in cpp or 'display' in cpp, 'Inventory class needs a displayAll method'
assert 'searchByName' in cpp or 'search_by_name' in cpp or 'search' in cpp, 'Inventory class needs a search method'
assert 'operator<<' in cpp or 'operator <<' in cpp, 'Overload the << operator for Product'
assert 'Inventory inventory' in cpp or 'Inventory inv' in cpp, 'Create an Inventory object in main'
acceptance_criteria: Inventory class defined with private vector<Product> member
Public methods: addProduct, removeProduct, displayAll, searchByName
operator<< overloaded for Product to enable cout << product
Menu creates an Inventory object and calls its methods
Remove Product option added to the menu
hints: Use products.push_back(p) to add and products.erase(products.begin() + i) to remove
The << overload is a friend or free function: ostream& operator<<(ostream& os, const Product& p)
In displayAll, loop through the vector and use cout << products[i] with your overloaded <<
vector<Product> grows automatically, so no need for capacity limits anymore
